content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for hidden-gem thriller/mystery movies with shocking truths, conspiracies, or reality-bending plots (already watched most popular recommendations)

**Score:** 54
**Body:** I'm looking for movie recommendations that are genuinely gripping and keep you thinking long after they end.

My favorite themes:

Mystery

 thriller

Hidden truths and secrets

Conspiracies

Reality isn't what it seems

Mind-bending concepts

Plot twists that completely change your understanding of the story

**A few of my favorites**:

The Sixth Sense

Prisoners

Interstellar

Inception

Memento

12 Monkeys

The Maze Runner

The Thirteenth Floor

The game

Predestination

The prestige 

Memories of murder 

Se7en

Zodiac 

Gone girl 

 etc

***I've already seen most of the commonly recommended titles***, so I'm especially interested in the lesser known that fit these themes. Can be recent movies or older ones 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [The Frame](https://reelgood.com/movie/the-frame-2014) - Brilliant indie film by Jamin Winans about a gang member who wants out and a paramedic who find themselves communicating via their tvs.  I always recommend this one to people who like *Coherence* or *Predestination*.  

[Stuart Bliss](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/262216-stuart-bliss) - It’s tagline is *An X-Files Episode on Meth*.... and yeah.  That's about right.  Not the best ever, but it kept our attention.

[World on a Wire (1973)](https://reelgood.com/movie/world-on-a-wire-1973) - Based on the same story as *The Thirteenth Floor* (*Simulacron-3* by Daniel Galouye), it takes a different look at the same story, where a computer scientist running a simulation of reality, investigates a death.

[The Quiet Earth](https://reelgood.com/movie/the-quiet-earth-1985) - One of my two favorite films of all time.  *Do not read any other reviews or descriptions or watch any previews!!!* They all give away major plot points. Even Reelgood and IMDB give away important information. All you need to know is that it is about a man who wakes up to find that everyone else has disappeared and that it was filmed and set in New Zealand in the mid-'80s. It is a phenomenal slow burn sf movie.

[Myth of Man](https://reelgood.com/movie/myth-of-man-2025) - The other one of my two favorite films of all time, it’s a masterpiece of visual storytelling.  It's Jamin Winans' ([Ink](https://reelgood.com/movie/ink-2009), [The Frame](https://reelgood.com/movie/the-frame-2014) (which I recommended above and is a better intro to his filmography)) newest film.  It follows a young woman in a dangerous steampunk world, who receives a message from her creator that leads her on an epic journey.  It massively over-performs its minuscule budget.  My buddy thought it had a budget 20 times its actual budget.  Be aware that Winans doesn't spell everything out for you and you will have to think about the film to get some of the details.

[Kill List](https://reelgood.com/movie/kill-list-2011) - Follows a hitman who is taking his first assignment after a yearlong hiatus due to a job that went really wrong.  For how unknown this film is, there are a *lot* of discussions about the ending.
  - These are all new for me 😲
  - Myth of Man is almost a religious experience and one of the greatest movies I have ever seen. I also understand it's not for everyone and it is not for casual/laundry folding viewing .
2. [removed]
  - Already watched these 3 and enjoyed too,
These are also highly recommended in reddit 
3. Fracture (2007)

The Invisible Guest (2016)

Angel Heart (1987)
4. Dead Again with Kenneth Branagh and Emma Thompson. Small role by Robin Williams.
5. Serenity (2019) -barely anyone saw this movie. neo-noir thriller kinda thing. Matthew McConaughey. Do yourself a favor and don’t look up the plot. It’s perfectly in your zone of “twist”. And is very weird. 

It’s technically a mini series, but you can watch the episodes together like a long movie:  The Langoliers (1995) - totally underrated Stephen King story . 
6. Border (2018) excellent Swedish film that's about 4-5 genres together, with one WTF reveal after another. Eva Melander gives an uncanny, emotionally powerful performance. The ending is unexpected and very satisfying.

Swimming Pool (2003) Charlotte Rampling and Ludivine Sagnier go toe to toe in this British-French film directed by the canny Francois Ozon. The way it ends is quite Hitchcockian. Rampling is mesmerizing, as always. 

Belle de Jour (1967) Famed surrealist artist and filmmaker Luis Bunuel directed and co-wrote this gorgeous perverse classic. It's one of the greatest films ever made, a bit lost to time now, though film geeks will know it. Catherine Deneuve is at her peak icy beauty, which adds to the jarring nature of how she decides to spend her days. A sexy, bizarre, at times hilarious masterpiece. 

La Jette (1962) this is a short gritty looking French sci fi film where the final scenes throw the entire film into perspective. 

Under the Skin (2013) ominous and mysterious, with a good portion of non actors and unscripted scenes. it gets creepier and more baffling as the story unfolds. The ending is a major jolt and unforgettable. I was so haunted by this film that I got the book it's based on. and damn, the novel is even more unnerving than the movie! I sometimes regret reading it because it's stuck in my mind now.
7. The Ghost Writer. 

## 2. Under the Skin is a masterpiece...

**Score:** 51
**Body:** ... unless you don't think so. If y'all are into slow, trippy, beautiful, and fucked up stories, Under the Skin could be Scarlet Johansson's greatest gift to cinema. I personally think it's best to go in without looking. When I say it's slow, it's fucking slow. It feels longer than it is. Two hours went by like three, and though the first half hour or so was good but not great, you'll eventually watch a scene that confirms to you that this is a masterpiece. Either that, or you spend  your time asking why you're putting up with it.

If you're a fan of the art and technique of movies, if you're a geek who likes to learn from other forms of arts and crafts, or if you respect movies that feel daring and new, Under the Skin is my recommendation for you.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Let’s not forget the soundtrack
  - What I love about it is that it feels much more like background noise than music. Whenever it kicks in, it could just as easily be diegetic as it is the score.
2. All of Jonathan Glazer’s work is fantastic. 
  - Yeah, I'ma have to look them up. Everything about this was just amazing.
    - def watch Birth. arguably Kidman's best performance.
3. Yes it is. It is also a film that is impossible to appreciate without a second viewing.
4. That beach scene will live in my head forever. Great movie
  - The beach scene and what followed was absolutely harrowing for me. I think my heart dropped. For me though, I wanted to cry for >!the scene with Adam Pearson, the man with the visible disability!<. The movie had me shifting through so many feelings for that. In general, the runtime was an exercise for me on emotional regulation.
5. favorite movie of all time
6. This movie changed me! I was obsessed when I saw it for the first time. I need to rewatch it soon.

## 3. the movie TUSK is next level horror and v under appreciated

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I compare it to SAW movies, and I think SAW gives the audience a framework to process the horror. Tusk offers no such thing. it's much more disturbing because it's pure dehumanization for the sake of the perpetrator's private fantasy. (a Kevin smith's film)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You should watch (and listen to) Bone Tomahawk..
2. I'm a pretty serious horror fan and I could barely stomach this one. I see what it was going for and I think the special effects work is phenomenal (and disgusting) but I found the viewing experience wholly unpleasant. 
  - There was too much comedy in *Tusk* for me to be repelled by the horror.  It more closely resembled a cartoon that can't possibly be true like *The Super Mario Galaxy Movie* rather than something that could be true like *Silence of the Lambs* or *Deliverance.*   
3. My buddy put this on for me to watch. He was freaked the fuck out by it. I, on the other hand was laughing my ass off through the whole thing. He actually got pissed off at me for laughing, but I thought it was way more comedy than horror. He turned it off and yelled at me. This was just a few years ago and we are both in our 50s haha! So one man's Horror can be another man's comedy. I did like the movie, it was funny as hell!
4. It was horrible. I mean the ending is one of the most ridiculous endings in the history of cinema. Parks as great in it with what he has to work with, and the Depp cameo was amusing. I heard the smodcast it was based on way back. 
5. Movies like Tusk or Funny Games are what I like to call "hopeless horror". It's often not known until the end but when you see something that is just irreversible misery; that's hopeless horror.
6. I will always vote, WALRUS YES!
7. I laughed my ass off and thoroughly enjoyed it in a room full of people who felt the exact opposite. 

It's a 10th Dentist type film 
8. I made my wife watch this ten years ago and she still brings it up as the most disturbing movie she’s ever seen. I was talking to her about Hokum on Sunday and asked if she wanted to watch it and her first question was “it’s not like the walrus movie is it?”
9. This movie really disturbed me. I couldn’t look at walruses, even stuffed animals, without feeling sick.

## 4. What’s your favourite pick-me-up movie when life feels a bit heavy?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I’ve been feeling a bit tired and unmotivated lately, and I’m looking for a movie that leaves you feeling better than when you started.

Not necessarily the funniest movie, but something uplifting, comforting, inspiring, or just genuinely enjoyable.

What’s your go-to pick-me-up movie, and why?

Thanks in advance! 🎬🍿

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shrek
2. My cousin Vinny
3. Kiki's Delivery Service (1989)
4. Moonstruck. All the performances are bonkers. Nicholas Cage and Cher are perfection.
5. Scott Pilgrim Vs the World
6. Holes 

Just introduced my kids to it last week and I’d forgotten just how good it is. Perseverance in difficult circumstances lessons all over the place. 
7. Bridesmaids
8. The Jimmy Stewart classic “Harvey” about Elwood P. Dodd (Stewart) and his best friend and drinking buddy Harvey, an invisible 7 foot tall magical rabbit. If you haven’t seen it, I can’t imagine anyone hating it. And for me, it’s perfect for the blues.
9. Moonrise Kingdom
10. harry potter

## 5. Looking for a movie thats genuinely traumatic.

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Hello. I'm looking for a movie that's raw. A movie that will genuinely fuck me up psychologically and emotionally. Not necessarily torture core or gore with lots of blood and stuff like Human Centipede or some goofy shit... But a movie with intentional storytelling. A movie that will have me rocking maniacally from trying to comprehend the sheer devastation I watched 

Think of Requiem For A Dream, Irreversible, Martyrs etc. 

I want something deep, something intentional. Something that'll bring a grown man close to tears

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Anything directed by Michael Haneke
2. Dancer in the Dark
3. Grave of the Fireflies
4. Manchester By The Sea
5. Come And See 

Come and See is a 1985 Soviet epic historical anti-war film directed by Elem Klimov and starring Aleksei Kravchenko and Olga Mironova.
6. Dear Zachary


That should ruin your week.
7. *Come And See* 
8. *Marley and Me*

That film is painful in a beautiful way.
9. i caught the last half of beau is afraid and it was so good at triggering my anxiety and trauma and shame and guilt i’m not sure i want to watch the whole thing 
10. We need to talk about Kevin.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Idris Elba Says Bond Rumors Were ‘Never Legit’ and ‘Not Realistic’: Audiences ‘Won’t Go for a Black Male Playing Bond’

**Score:** 3538

![gif](giphy|SYABnw07wJsV7G8ju5)

2. I don't think people remember how pissed people were at "James blonde" when Craig was cast
3. While a great actor, I think when the rumors came out he was still Luther and will a dynamic serial character, it is always hard to see someone with a well-developed role play another extremely well-developed character.
  - He's Stringer Bell to me
    - Stringer Bell side note: 

I'm on my 4th rewatch and I swear every scene he's in he says "close the door", it always makes me chuckle. Growing up a rough city myself (Detroit not Baltimore), "close the door" was probably the phrase I've heard the most in my life from adults .  That show has so many little things that make it relatable, especially if you're from the inner city.
4. Plus hes too old for the role

## 2. What is the your favorite portrayal of Satan, the Devil, or any equivalent figure in movies or television?

**Score:** 1610
**Body:** Tim Curry as Darkness is my all time favorite.

Other honorable mentions are Peter Storemare in Constantine and Jack Nicholson in The Witches of Eastwick.

## 3. What's a movie you really enjoyed, but its surprisingly low ratings left you baffled? For me, it was Event Horizon.

**Score:** 1601
**Body:** The visual design, cast, and blend of sci-fi with supernatural elements made it really enjoyable. I watched it quite late without knowing anything about it, so its low ratings really confused me. It was pure fun.

It’s one of my favorite superhero films and Guillermo Del Toro directed it so it has his aesthetic all over it.

4. Event Horizon is one of my favorite movies. I saw it in the theater and it scared the hell out of me. I watch it once every two years or so and most of it holds up.

Looks cheesy af.

2. I’m guessing it ends up a lot like Dungeons and Dragons, underrated and beloved.
  - The fact so many people are comparing it to that makes me want to go see it.
    - Saw it today. Super surprised by how entertaining it is. Fully expected garbage. 
      - It was so entertaining.  Sure it had its cheasy and campy moments but I fully appreciated and enjoyed them all.  Everything was written with that purpose, not to be taken seriously or try very hard. 

Skeletor was just fantastic.  Menacing but hilarious. .  That 'mind-scene' towards the end of the movie? Chef's Kiss.
3. Oh it’s for Gen X grownups to take their 7 yr olds.  And sign me up! I have the power!!!
4. I just watched it today and it was amazing. I’ve been waiting a couple decades for a new MOTU movie and I felt like they did a great job. Even my wife who has no idea who any of the people are thought it was a good movie. 
  - It’s an easy target to make fun of for cheap karma on here, but…

…damned if every review I’ve seen didn’t wind up being overwhelmingly positive. We just might have a good He-Man film on our hands, folks.
5. The movie was awesome. It was straight up fun and a great summer movie.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. How do you think future films should explore postmodern anxieties, especially those that are prominent in 2020's culture?

**Score:** 16
**Body:** With the recent success of *Backrooms*, which, in my interpretation recalls films like *Chain*, *Ghostbox Cowboy*, and even something like *Koyaanisqatsi* in unflinchingly looking at consumerism, extensive liminal spaces, neoliberal promises and failures, and disassociation and isolation from other people (although that's obviously not the reason for why it's successful), I feel like the general moviegoing audience is starting to become more interested in films that immerse themselves in what current life truly feels like, especially with how political/social/economic dynamics are merging to an intense level, and I think even platforms like Tiktok/IG Reels are contributing to this denseness, through Gen Z/Gen Alpha watching an AI parody of world politics --> debates between only fans models and politicians, etc. 

I hear the claim a lot that *Southland Tales* would be a huge hit nowadays, if only for its hyperreal presentation of culture slamming against itself. I don't necessarily know if a new wave of movies like this would be incredible at the box office, but those who do watch it would definitely champion them as courageous and of-the-moment.

 My question is: What would you actually like to see in films trying to tackle these issues? Some people think it's corny or out of touch when something like *Euphoria* S3 is trying to "capture the American rot", so does it come from an indie or outsider mindset? Films like *Eddington* capture AI Data Centers, Politics on Social media, but even a film like that was also considered cowardly. I'm mostly trying to figure out what people would want of a film of this ambition, and what steps it should take to become a cultural touchstone/ signifier of our culture. What do you guys think?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'd like to see some more films that engage with the workplace through the lens of the work of Byung Chul Han and the self-exploitation type stuff. The closest I've seen was No Other Choice, which I still feel has been a bit underrated and not talked about as much as it could have been. 
2. I watched *Southland Tales* for the first time earlier this year and found it so profoundly prescient and affecting for precisely the reasons you mentioned. Even the weird acting sort of fit the plots' themes, insofar as Dwayne Johnson and JT looking like they didnt know wtf was happening fit the general dazed anarchic confusion that seems typical of our era. 

I think my ideal film rn would be something as crazy and meme-surrealistic as *Southland Tales* with some of the better formal innovations of Peter Greenaway's *Tulse Luper* films, combined maybe with some of the extreme cutting and anti-choreography styles you get (accidentally?) in Michael Bay films and (intentionally?) in Safdie brothers' earlier stuff. 

I don't think you need to tackle these issues by literally representing them in film and having the story be ABOUT them. Often some distance is more effective, and having these issues deeply inspire your stylistic and content choices can be more effective imo. 

The main things are post-truth, internet surrealism, info-overload, digital omni-opticon, explosion of contradictory perspectives, contrast between Silicon utopia and post 70s economic reality. Also short-form content, montage, needle-drops, lyrical epiphanies, pastiche, endless self-reflexivity.
  - Have you seen *Sorry To Bother You*? Seems up your alley. 
3. I feel inferior as a movie buff cuz I haven't seen any of these movies you mention, except Koyaanisqatsi. I do plan to watch Backrooms.   
I don't know why but movies about AI and social media feel contrived and tired already. Maybe because it's such low hanging fruit and doesn't really deal with the true implications and just uses it as a gimmick for some B-horror movie.   
I haven't seen Euphoria S3 yet, and not sure I will because it looks so fake and forced compared to the first two seasons. Those were extremely good despite being a very unrealistic heightened look at American high school life. The emotions and underlying issues were told in a very sincere and compelling way.   
Movies with messages about the dangers of AI and capitalism have already been made 50 years ago, like Terminator or Blade Runner, we just didn't listen. Making more movies about it after the fact seems insincere and repetitive. 

What I'd want to see is more sincerity and sensitivity, something that shows the real human impact, like the disassociation and isolation you mention, not just someone getting killed by an AI doing a funny TikTok dance.   
I just commented on another post about Civil War (2024), that was I felt a really important criticism of the current social division and what it could lead to, also showing its tragic personal impact beyond something sensationalist. 'The Boys' is very heavy-handed, but intentionally so, and also for the most part gives a very sincere criticism of current events when everyone else is concerned with only making palatable mediocrity so it can be shared on social media without getting censored. And I wouldn't want it to all be about politics and division, it can also be about more small-scale tragedy and hardship and the beauty that can still come out of such things, like Euphoria seasons 1-2.

On a sidenote, do you think Euphoria S3 is still worth watching?
  - I want a sitcom of a prosecutor office’s misdemeanor docket. You’d see the small scale tragedy, the things that actually affect people’s lives. 
    - Personally I'm never a fan of legal settings, but if it's done as well as Scrubs was I'd watch it. Hospital shows are great for this. Speaking of, though not a sitcom The Pitt is a great recent example of realistic and real human tragedy. Also on relatively small scale, except the one major event in the first season.
    - It would be rad if they did it like The Wire style anti-copaganda.   
Like an episode starts off with a prosecutor “showing discretion” ie planning to drop a case against a low level but repeat offender defendant  because the charge is a nothingburger and it gives them leverage over the defense with a mor important case. But then by the end of the episode, in order to help out another staff member (for ethical reasons) and therby uphold the integrity of the office; they end up getting bargained the defendant back and violate his parole which gives him 3 strikes and sends him away for 15 years for trying to return an xbox with a doctored receipt. 
4. The first thought that came to my mind was the movie Parasite. I had a visceral reaction to that movie and I think the same was true for a lot of people in the United States; which is ironic being a Korean film. It exposed out in the open, hidden truths about class in society and self worth, and the tragedy of struggling just to get by; the loss of dignity one may feel on the clock or hustling and having a superior reprimand you for not being enthusiastic enough about your work. 

So yes this a film from 2019, but it would be neat to see more films in the future that address these modern class struggles including disparities in wealth and the violence of systematic poverty. But done so in such a smart and psychological and visceral fashion. To get to the heart of what it feels like and what people are afraid to say. Don’t Look Up would be another example. 
  - No Other Choice is a Korean film from last year which based on your comment you may find quite interesting 

## 2. The Platform is a bloody ride through the classes of the society!

**Score:** 17
**Body:** It is really one of the most brutal, claustrophobic thrillers about wealth distribution I have ever seen. 

The script's engine is incredibly straightforward but devastating: the levels above gorge themselves and waste the food, while the levels below eat each other to death. 

It is the clearest, ugliest mirror of our own society. It's so realistic. You'll immediately feel for the plight of the paupers and the comfort of the elites in the society just by watching it completely.

The performances are perfectly subtle. Everyone looks genuinely isolated and starving, and the film pushes the human instinct for survival to terrifying, realistic heights. You don't need heavy exposition when the physical mechanics of a descending table do all the heavy lifting.

The set, environment, cinematography and lighting makes this movie more amazing, heightening the overall claustrophobic nature of the scenes. It's not an “Everyday Prison”. It's a pit, an arena where you have to eat your way out to win.

But you can feel the exact moment the script loses its nerve.

For the first two acts, the subtext is brilliant. Then the third act completely floods the engine. It abandons its grounded survival mechanics and turns into a heavy-handed, symbolic "Messiah" allegory. 

Honestly the third act is the biggest problem in the movie that kinda demolishes everything so brilliantly built.

The internal logic completely breaks down, especially the storyline with Miharu and her child, which remains frustratingly unexplained and physically impossible within the rules the movie established. How was that child surviving in the deepest pit? Why didn't they freeze or get hot due to extra food in the pit? Why was the administration lying about the child rules? The script just fell flat and went against its own, strictly established rules.

It also leaves a lot of narrative anchors completely vague. We never really find out why Goreng risked his life in the hole just for a diploma, and the Administration is left entirely faceless and silent. While that vague bureaucracy works to show how little the top cares about the bottom, it still leaves the narrative feeling slightly incomplete.

But despite the script trading its mechanical logic for heavy symbolism in the final stretch, it ends on a perfect cliffhanger. 

It is a terrifying blueprint, a perfection, and it absolutely did not need a sequel.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >Honestly the third act is the biggest problem in the movie that kinda demolishes everything so brilliantly built.


>The internal logic completely breaks down



Idk man. You might be right in a sense, its been a while since I watched it. But I would say I didnt have that reaction to the 3rd act at all and that also makes total sense to me.



On its face the whole movie is just super allegorical and thus nonsensical. The whole idea of why someone would build a prison like that is just very nonsensical.


I hear you when you say "strict rules the movie established", but obviously it seems the film makers were then not as concerned with that as you are.


I would maybe just put on my "benevolent critic" hat and ask yourself what the film makers were trying to do in the third act.


In a sense it seems oddly coherent exactly in that incoherence. The third act is the act of "revolution". Does it not make sense that this is where all the rules get thrown out of the window, even the rules of physics, of film making, of everything?


Empathizing with it, it seems thats what they were kind of going for. You could say its to their credit that they are able to have their feets in both modi: The first 2 acts are all serious and orderly and coherent, in line with the message. In the 3rd act they switch to some highly intuitive, avantgarde, irrationalist mode. You are not meant to make sense of some stupid physics about some stupid child!!! The child is a symbol, man!!! This is the revolution!!! Everything up will be down and everything down will be up!! We are doing it, man, we are doing it!!!"


Something like that, yes? If you are such a fan of the movie, I would encourage you to try and rewatch the movie and the 3rd act through those eyes. Just accept that in the 3rd act they switch to some kind of Jodorowsky type of film making, and doing that is kind of coherent with their general message. The 3rd act is very narratively dense, there are a lot of allusions being made to all kinds of leftist ideology. It just wont be as sensical as the first 2 acts. If you can accept that you might appreciate the third act too.
  - I like it think its better until the old man died , and I lack enoungh context and ref for it.

anyone know a porper piece to anaysis it or  rather talk about real events that trigger the fragments in the film?
2. I love this movie so much and agree wholeheartedly with everything you’ve said. If you watch The Platform 2 (I think that’s what it’s called?), it does even more work undoing the things that anchor the first 2 acts of The Platform. 

## 3. Suzume (2022) Review

**Score:** 9
**Body:** I watched Suzume today, and I didn't think this movie would make me emotional. The setup was such that I thought in the end Souta would become fine, they would find a way to close all the doors, and the movie would have a happy ending. But then we are shown Daijin’s POV, and then finally that ending where Suzume meets her younger self. That was extremely emotional. The movie’s animation and music were amazing, just like every other Makoto Shinkai movie, but this time the story was quite different. 9/10 in my opinion. 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love this movie. The sky over Tokyo makes me tear up every time. As someone who grew up in a seismically stable part of the world, it really brought home to me the quiet horror of living in a place where the land itself might try to kill you
2. I’m in the camp that thinks this film is generally underrated. While a bit repetitive at times, i don’t think i’ve come across another film with action sequences so deeply imbued with emotional resonance. It’s by no means a perfect movie but I think it’s quite the achievement.
3. I’m going to be honest but I really didn’t like this film, especially the aspects of Suzume wanting to throw away everything for Souta when they’d spent all of a week together not even. I know some people get hung up on their age difference (which becomes even more of an issue to some degree when you find out about the arrest/jailing of the producer of this film, because it does put an extra layer on top of the romance in the film that makes you look at it a bit differently once you know that.) I liked the animation and thought that was impressive, but overall the story just didn’t do much for me and I felt it needed another rewrite or two to tighten it up. 

## 4. One of the few pieces of LYNCH/ERASERHEAD memorabilia in existence.

**Score:** 6
**Body:** I wanted to share this with the community because I think fellow Lynch fans will appreciate it more than most.

I'm auctioning an original letter from David Lynch to actor Jack Walsh regarding his work on Eraserhead.

The provenance is direct. Jack Walsh personally gave me the letter. Jack was a friend and collaborator of mine, and I worked with him on multiple occasions over the years.

For those unfamiliar with Jack, he played Mr. Roundheels in Eraserhead and later appeared in The Straight Story, making him one of the relatively small group of actors who were part of Lynch's creative world across multiple decades. Jack was also the “He’s got your baby” Ghost in Insidious Chapter 2

What makes this piece special is that it isn't simply an autograph or publicity item. It is direct correspondence from David Lynch to a performer who helped bring Eraserhead to life.

Eraserhead was David Lynch's first feature film and has become one of the defining and most influential films of the twentieth century. Its impact can be felt across independent cinema, horror, surrealism, music videos, and popular culture as a whole. Despite the film's enormous cultural significance, relatively little personal correspondence, production material, or ephemera directly connected to the making of Eraserhead surfaces publicly. Most of it remains in private hands, institutional collections, or has simply been lost to time.

That's part of what makes this letter so remarkable to me. It isn't just connected to David Lynch; it's connected to Eraserhead itself and to one of the actors who helped create it. It's a tangible link to the people behind a landmark work of American independent cinema.

As a filmmaker, I've gone back and forth about keeping it. Ultimately, I've decided it deserves to find a home with a serious Lynch collector, film historian, archive, museum, or fan who understands its significance and will preserve it for the future.

Photos are below. Auction link is [https://www.juliensauctions.com/en/items/2249445/david-lynch-signed-letter-to-jack-walsh-regarding-eraserhead](https://www.juliensauctions.com/en/items/2249445/david-lynch-signed-letter-to-jack-walsh-regarding-eraserhead)

I'm happy to answer questions about the provenance, my relationship with Jack, or the letter itself.

## 5. It's an ELI5 question, I always wondered why there are so much names sometimes as the writers of certain movies. Why and how can so much people work/co-write on the same movie?

**Score:** 6
**Body:** You have some movies where there are a lot of names credited in the writing area. For eg, Universal Soldier is written by Richard Rothstein, Christopher Leitch and Dean Devlin!

Mad Max 2 was written by Terry Hayes, George Miller and Brian Hannant. Vampire in Brooklyn's screenplay was made by Charles Q. Murphy, Michael Lucker, Chris Parker and story written by Eddie Murphy, Vernon Lynch, Charles Q. Murphy. Angel Has Fallen, screenplay by Robert Mark Kamen, Matt Cook, Ric Roman Waugh and the story is from Creighton Rothenberger and Katrin Benedikt.

Sometimes filmmakers are involved too and co-write their movies : Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me was written by David Lynch Robert Engels. Independance Day by Roland Emmerich and Dean Devlin. 

In situation like this how everyone is working? And is there any proof or interviews that when the writers are maybe sitting together in a room and where they would all work together/give ideas/write etc

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. When the credit lists the writers with an "&" they are a writing team. When the credit uses the word "and" it means several different writers/teams worked on it. Who gets more credit and what order that goes in gets decided/arbitrated by the guild.
2. Writing processes for films are often very drawn out and very messy. Many names could mean that the film went through several rewrites, with different writers or teams of writers working on each draft, so although the names might be all grouped together in the credits, a lot of those people never actually worked together and in fact worked on different versions/stages of the film. Studio gets a draft from a writer, takes it and hands it to a different writer and asks them to do a rewrite on it, then again, etc. And in the end they all have to be credited. With big Hollywood films this is often the case.  It also sometime has to do with credit, certain contributions entitle you to a writing credit as per WGA, even if you never wrote an entire draft.
  - Adding onto this, at the end of the day the WGA decides what counts as writing a movie in a way that doesn’t have consistent rules. Sometimes actors writing/rewriting lines on set for comedies is enough to count, often times it isn’t. Infamously in the case of the first Scary Movie, the Weinsteins bought two similar scripts for a slasher movie parody, and the WGA decided that the writers of both scripts needed to be credited, even though the Wayans brothers are adamant that not a single joke from the other script was used in the film. 
3. When it comes to actors like Eddie Murphy, sometimes they get a writing credit simply because of improv's on set, other actors who have a very particular 'way' about them like Will Smith might be involved with rewrites that simply involving making it more 'them' in the script when it was originally written with someone else in mind.

It is not also out of the realm of possibility that with Vampire in Brooklyn, Eddie was given a writing credit because his brothers wrote the script but no one was going to read it unless they put his name on it. The Wiki page says 'wrote with his brothers' but shortly after says his brothers wrote it, adding his name on the cover is going to get people to read it.

Some writing credits can literally be there because they created the world or characters and they had absolutely nothing to do with the project other than they wrote the first movie.

Most of the time a script with go thru many peoples hands before it ends up on the screen, sometimes it is one person who finishes the original script, sometimes they are writing it but they have a buddy they use as a sounding board which might not actually write anything on the page but they were instrumental to helping figure the writer what actually ends up on the page. William Wisher is credited with 'additional dialogue' for Terminator, James Cameron was sleeping on his couch at the time while writing the script and they would frequently discuss aspects of the story, script etc and Wisher would make suggestions and help rework ideas into the finished project so it is not like he simply provided one line to the script, who knows how the script would have turned out without that sounding board, but he might consider it as not 'writing the script' simply because his buddy was talking to him over breakfast.
4. Writing credits are specifically and strictly outlined by the WGA. I believe you can look up the criteria. 

Directors will get a credit for very little input. You can't assume that a shared credit means they originated a substantial amount of the script on their own. 

Scripts get revised continuously sometimes up until the moment the dialogue is spoken by an actor. They change scenes, lines etc. constantly. Its important to note also that not everyone that has a hand in this process is actually credited.
5. Unlike, say, novels, movies cost a huge amount of money to produce, and so therefore only happen if many people with money or who are valuable to people with money say yes. What that means is an original idea could get people excited, but various producers, studio executives, prominent actors, or prominent directors could see the promise in the idea, but have their own opinions about how it should play out. That could lead to new writers being brought on board. 

Even aside from that, a writer may just not do a good job, leading to another writer being hired. The result is that for many movies, particularly studio movies,there are many people who have written parts of it. In fact, for major studio movies, it’s very likely that there are a bunch of other writers who aren’t credited at all, but weighed in on earlier drafts, were hired specifically as script doctors who wouldn’t be credited, etc.
